worth_it_explanation: Yahoo DSP (demand-side platform) reporting surfaces numbers that require active interpretation to be useful: total conversions blend click-through and view-through attributions into a single figure, measurable impressions differ from total impressions delivered, and the platform has no direct connection to the advertiser's analytics or ad server. A team that reads Yahoo DSP numbers without a reconciliation process, a regular review cadence, or an understanding of what each metric actually counts will draw conclusions from data they have not verified. Each of these checks is a process and settings review, not a rebuild.
intro: Yahoo DSP (demand-side platform) reporting covers delivery, performance, and attribution within the platform's own measurement system. Because Yahoo DSP has no direct connection to an advertiser's analytics platform or third-party ad server, the numbers it surfaces need to be read in context. 'Total conversions' combines click-through and view-through attributions into one figure; 'measurable impressions' counts only the subset of delivered impressions where viewability was technically trackable, not the full count of impressions billed or served. When these distinctions are not understood, and when no regular review cadence exists, the team acts on numbers it has not verified. These checks confirm that Yahoo DSP reporting is reconciled, reviewed on a schedule, and read with accurate metric definitions in place.

audit_checks.title: Yahoo DSP-reported conversions are not reconciled against the advertiser's analytics or ad server
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Yahoo DSP counts conversions using its own attribution system, which combines click-through and view-through attributions into a single total. An advertiser's analytics platform counts sessions and goals using a different method. When no reconciliation process exists, the team has two different conversion numbers with no way to determine which is closer to reality or why they differ. Decisions about budget, bidding, and creative are made from an unverified number.

educate.base:
Yahoo DSP (demand-side platform) tracks conversions through two distinct attribution paths. Click-through conversions occur when a user clicks an ad and then completes a conversion action on the advertiser's site. View-through conversions occur when a user sees an ad impression but does not click, and later completes a conversion action within the attribution window. Yahoo DSP's default reporting surfaces a 'Conversions' total that combines both types into one figure.

An advertiser's analytics platform, such as Google Analytics 4 (GA4) or Adobe Analytics, counts conversions through its own session and goal-tracking logic. It typically requires a click and a direct or referred session to attribute a conversion, and it does not count view-through attributions at all. As a result, the analytics platform will almost always report fewer conversions than Yahoo DSP for the same campaign period. That gap is not necessarily a sign that either number is wrong; it is a sign that they are counting different things.

A third-party ad server, when present, adds a third count using its own click and impression tracking. Differences between the ad server and Yahoo DSP figures trace to technical timing differences in how each system fires and records impressions and clicks, as well as differences in how each handles ad blockers, browser privacy settings, and tag loading order.

The Microsoft Monetize and Xandr discrepancy framework, which applies directly to Yahoo DSP buyers since Yahoo DSP purchases inventory through Microsoft's supply infrastructure, identifies 10 percent as the threshold above which impression discrepancy between two systems warrants investigation, and 15 percent for mobile inventory. Conversion discrepancy between Yahoo DSP and an analytics platform will almost always exceed that range because the attribution methodologies are structurally different, but documenting the expected gap and monitoring whether it widens is the practice that catches real configuration problems.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The team reports Yahoo DSP conversions and analytics conversions in separate documents without noting the expected difference between them or what accounts for it
  • The Yahoo DSP conversion total is used directly in client reporting without noting that it includes view-through attributions
  • No one on the team has mapped the Yahoo DSP attribution window settings against the analytics platform's session timeout to understand the structural source of the gap
You may want to document the expected conversion gap between Yahoo DSP and your analytics platform before using either number in external reporting, and note the view-through attribution share in the Yahoo DSP total so stakeholders understand what is being claimed.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an online store, Yahoo DSP may report a purchase conversion for a user who saw a display ad and later returned to the site through a search or direct visit within the view-through window. The analytics platform records the same purchase as a search or direct conversion with no ad attribution. You may want to confirm whether the client's reporting treats Yahoo DSP view-through conversions as incremental or whether they are likely being counted elsewhere in the attribution stack.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, a view-through conversion credited by Yahoo DSP for a form submission may be attributed to organic or paid search in the analytics platform if the user did not click the Yahoo ad. You may want to confirm with the client which attribution model they use for performance evaluation before presenting Yahoo DSP conversion totals as the primary performance number.

learn_more.source_title: Prepare to request a report discrepancy investigation (Microsoft Monetize)

educate.learn_more_note: Yahoo DSP conversion metric definitions, including the distinction between click-through and view-through attribution and the configurable attribution windows for each, are documented in the Yahoo DSP Help Center, which requires a Yahoo DSP account login to access (help.yahooinc.com/dsp). Contact your Yahoo DSP account team for attribution window guidance specific to your advertiser setup.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether a reconciliation process exists between Yahoo DSP conversion reporting and the advertiser's analytics platform or ad server, and establish the expected gap between the two counts.

yahoo_dsp_ui.label: Yahoo DSP UI
steps: Select 'Advertisers' in the left navigation pane and choose the advertiser you are auditing.
Navigate to the Reports section. Open the most recent campaign performance report. Locate the 'Conversions' total for the campaign period you are reviewing.

Note whether the report distinguishes click-through conversions from view-through conversions, or whether a single combined total is shown. If only a combined total is visible, ask the team whether they have access to a breakdown by attribution type in a custom report.

Pull the same date range from the advertiser's analytics platform (e.g., GA4, Adobe Analytics). Compare the analytics conversion total against the Yahoo DSP total for the same campaign period. Calculate the percentage gap: (Yahoo DSP total minus analytics total) divided by the Yahoo DSP total.
If a third-party ad server is in use, pull its conversion count for the same period and compare it against Yahoo DSP as a second data point.
Ask the team whether the expected gap between Yahoo DSP and analytics was documented at the campaign's start, and whether the current gap falls within or outside that expected range.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the Yahoo DSP conversion total, the analytics platform conversion total, the percentage gap between them, and whether a documented expected range exists. A campaign with a documented expected gap and a current gap within that range passes. A campaign with no documented expected gap, or with a current gap that has grown significantly relative to prior periods without an identified cause, is a fail recorded with the Yahoo DSP total, the analytics total, the percentage gap, and whether view-through attribution is included in the Yahoo DSP figure.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Yahoo DSP conversion total, the analytics conversion total, and the percentage gap for the campaign period above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Yahoo DSP (demand-side platform) conversion reporting is not reconciled against the advertiser's analytics platform. Yahoo DSP combines click-through and view-through conversions into a single total using its own attribution windows, while the analytics platform counts only sessions that include a direct click interaction. The two systems will always produce different numbers by design, but without a documented expected gap, a growing discrepancy looks the same as a stable one. {{voice_recommend}} documenting the expected conversion gap between Yahoo DSP and the analytics platform at the start of each campaign, separating click-through and view-through attributions in Yahoo DSP reporting so stakeholders understand what the total includes, and flagging periods where the gap changes significantly relative to the established baseline.

audit_checks.title: No scheduled reporting cadence exists, so pacing and performance problems are identified late
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Yahoo DSP does not send proactive delivery or performance alerts by default beyond its AI-driven troubleshooting agent's findings. A team that pulls reports only at campaign end has no opportunity to correct an underpacing line, a creative that stopped delivering, or a line spending faster than planned before the damage is done. Catching these conditions mid-flight requires a reporting schedule.

educate.base:
Yahoo DSP (demand-side platform) provides delivery and performance data in near real-time through its reporting interface, but it does not automatically surface all delivery problems to the practitioner. Pacing deviations, creative eligibility issues, and bid competitiveness gaps may be identified by Yahoo DSP's AI-driven troubleshooting agent, but they are not sent as alerts unless the practitioner actively monitors the platform or has configured notification preferences available in the account.

The consequence of an irregular review cadence is that problems compound before they are caught. A line that begins underpacing in week one of a four-week flight will fall further behind each day the issue is not addressed. By week three, recovering spend to budget requires more aggressive bidding, which raises effective cost per impression above what the flight assumed. A line that has exhausted budget in week two of the same flight delivers nothing in weeks three and four, a problem that is obvious only in retrospect without mid-flight checks.

A reporting cadence for an active campaign should cover, at a minimum: campaign-level pacing relative to the flight budget and elapsed time, line-level delivery status, creative performance by format, and any anomalous changes in cost per mille (CPM) or click-through rate (CTR) that may signal an inventory or targeting shift. Checking weekly is a reasonable baseline for most campaigns; campaigns in the final third of their flight benefit from checks every two to three days when recovery options are still available.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The team's first view of campaign delivery data for a completed flight is the end-of-flight report, with no documented mid-flight reviews
  • There is no standing meeting, shared dashboard, or recurring task tied to Yahoo DSP campaign monitoring during active flights
  • A prior flight ended with significant underspend or overspend that was not identified until after the flight closed
You may want to establish a written cadence for how often active campaigns are reviewed and who is responsible for pulling each report type before the next flight launches.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a store running a promotional flight tied to a sale window, a delivery problem identified on the last day of the promotion cannot be corrected in time to recover impressions. You may want to schedule delivery reviews at least every two to three days during a promotional flight, with an additional check in the first 24 to 48 hours after launch to confirm lines are delivering as expected.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser with a fixed monthly budget, a line that underspends in week one and week two leaves the team with an inflated remaining budget that is difficult to spend efficiently in week three and four without relaxing targeting or raising bids above plan. You may want to review pacing weekly and note any line running more than 10 percentage points behind its expected pace midpoint.

educate.learn_more_note: Yahoo DSP provides campaign monitoring and reporting tools within its platform interface. Documentation on available report types, delivery dashboards, and notification settings is available in the Yahoo DSP Help Center, which requires a Yahoo DSP account login to access (help.yahooinc.com/dsp). Contact your Yahoo DSP account team for guidance on setting up delivery alerts or automated reporting exports.

impactful_updates.update_title: Yahoo DSP Agentic AI troubleshooting agent
impactful_updates.update_description: Yahoo DSP introduced an always-on AI troubleshooting agent as part of its agentic capabilities. The agent identifies delivery problems including underpacing, targeting constraints, and supply gaps, and recommends or executes corrective actions with human approval. This reduces the window between when a delivery problem begins and when the practitioner is made aware of it, but it does not replace a practitioner-owned reporting cadence for verifying pacing, creative performance, and cost trends.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether a written reporting cadence exists for active campaigns and whether mid-flight delivery reviews are documented.

yahoo_dsp_ui.label: Yahoo DSP UI
steps: Select 'Advertisers' in the left navigation pane and choose the advertiser you are auditing.

Navigate to the Campaigns page. For any campaign that completed in the last 90 days, check whether mid-flight delivery data is available in prior report exports or a shared dashboard. Note whether the team has documentation showing when reports were pulled during the flight.

For any currently active campaign, open the campaign delivery chart. Note whether the current delivery level matches the expected pace given the flight budget and elapsed time. A campaign with 50 percent of its budget spent at 60 percent of its flight elapsed is underpacing.
Ask the team what their standard reporting review schedule is for active campaigns. Confirm whether reviews happen weekly, bi-weekly, or only at campaign end.
Check whether Yahoo DSP's notification or alert settings are configured to flag delivery anomalies. Note any standing alerts that have been set up.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a written reporting cadence exists, how frequently active campaigns are reviewed, and whether mid-flight review records are available for the most recent completed flights. An account with a documented cadence of at least weekly delivery reviews on active campaigns and evidence of those reviews having occurred passes. An account where mid-flight reviews are not documented or where the first delivery review on a completed flight occurred at campaign end is a fail, recorded with the campaign name and the review frequency observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the current reporting review frequency, whether a written cadence exists, and whether mid-flight review records are available for recent flights above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Yahoo DSP (demand-side platform) campaigns are not reviewed on a regular cadence during active flights. Without scheduled mid-flight delivery checks, pacing problems, creative delivery gaps, and cost anomalies are identified only at campaign end, when corrective action is no longer possible. {{voice_recommend}} establishing a written reporting cadence covering delivery pacing, line-level spend, and creative performance at least weekly for active campaigns, with more frequent checks in the final third of each flight when underpacing is most costly to correct.

audit_checks.title: Yahoo DSP metrics are misread: measurable impressions used as total delivery, or total conversions treated as fully click-driven
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Reading measurable impressions as the full delivery count understates total impressions served and overstates the viewability rate because the denominator in the viewability calculation is only the subset of impressions where measurement was technically possible. Reading total conversions as if they were all click-driven overstates the directness of the campaign's impact since view-through conversions are included without requiring a click. Both misreads produce performance claims that the underlying data does not support.

educate.base:
Yahoo DSP (demand-side platform) surfaces two distinct impression counts in its reporting. 'Total impressions' is the number of ad impressions delivered across the campaign. 'Measurable impressions' is the subset of those impressions where viewability measurement was technically possible. Not every delivered impression can be measured for viewability: some publisher environments, ad formats, or page configurations do not support the measurement tag, so measurement is unavailable for that impression. Measurable impressions will always be fewer than or equal to total impressions.

Using measurable impressions as a proxy for total delivery understates how many impressions were served and produces an inflated viewability rate because the viewability percentage is calculated only against the impressions where measurement was technically possible, not against total delivery. If 2 million impressions were served, 1.6 million were measurable, and 1.2 million of those were viewable, the viewability rate is 75 percent of measurable impressions, not 60 percent of total delivery. The distinction matters when the campaign's reported viewability rate is used to justify creative or placement decisions.

Yahoo DSP's conversion reporting combines two attribution types in its default 'Conversions' figure. Click-through conversions credit a conversion to the campaign because the user clicked the ad before converting. View-through conversions credit a conversion to the campaign because the user saw the ad impression within the attribution window and later converted without clicking. The total is the sum of both. When a team reports 'total conversions' in a client deliverable without noting the view-through share, the number implies a level of direct response engagement the campaign may not have produced through clicks alone.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The campaign report uses 'measurable impressions' as the total delivery figure and calculates cost per mille (CPM) or reach against it rather than against total impressions
  • Viewability rate is calculated as viewable impressions divided by total impressions rather than as viewable impressions divided by measurable impressions, which is the correct denominator
  • Client reporting shows a 'conversions' total with no note distinguishing click-through from view-through attributions, and the number is used to calculate return on ad spend (ROAS) or cost per acquisition (CPA)
You may want to confirm which impression and conversion columns are in each standard report template before the next campaign launches, and add column-level notes explaining what each metric counts.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a store measuring ROAS from a Yahoo DSP campaign, a 'total conversions' figure that includes view-through attributions will produce a higher apparent ROAS than click-through conversions alone would show. You may want to confirm whether the ROAS number used in performance reporting is based on total conversions or click-through conversions only, and note the distinction in any external report that quotes the figure.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser measuring cost per lead (CPL) from a Yahoo DSP campaign, including view-through conversions in the denominator will lower the apparent CPL compared to a click-only count. You may want to confirm the CPL figure used for budget decisions reflects the attribution scope the client agreed to at campaign setup.

learn_more.source_title: Prepare to request a report discrepancy investigation (Microsoft Monetize)

educate.learn_more_note: Definitions of Yahoo DSP impression types (total impressions, measurable impressions, viewable impressions) and conversion attribution types (click-through conversions, view-through conversions) are documented in the Yahoo DSP Help Center, which requires a Yahoo DSP account login to access (help.yahooinc.com/dsp). Contact your Yahoo DSP account team to confirm the metric definitions in use in your account's reporting configuration.

investigate.intro: Review the columns present in the active campaign reporting template and confirm that total impressions, measurable impressions, and conversion attribution types are correctly labeled and interpreted.

yahoo_dsp_ui.label: Yahoo DSP UI
steps: Select 'Advertisers' in the left navigation pane and choose the advertiser you are auditing.

Navigate to the Reports section. Open the current standard campaign performance report template. Note whether the report includes separate columns for 'Total impressions' and 'Measurable impressions', or whether only one impression column is present.

If viewability rate is reported, confirm which denominator is in use: viewable impressions divided by measurable impressions is the correct calculation. Note whether the report or any downstream documentation calculates viewability against total impressions instead.
Locate the conversion column or columns in the report. Confirm whether the report distinguishes click-through conversions from view-through conversions, or whether only a combined total is shown.

Review any client-facing report, export, or slide that references Yahoo DSP conversions from the most recent flight. Note whether the conversion figure is labeled with its attribution scope (click-through only, view-through only, or combined), or whether it is presented as a plain number without that context.

Note the campaign's return on ad spend (ROAS) or cost per acquisition (CPA) calculation if present. Confirm which conversion figure and which impression figure were used as inputs to those calculations.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the active report template separates total impressions from measurable impressions, whether viewability rate is calculated against the correct denominator, and whether conversion totals distinguish click-through from view-through attribution. A report template that separates impression types, uses measurable impressions as the viewability denominator, and labels or separates click-through from view-through conversions passes. A report template that uses measurable impressions as the delivery total, calculates viewability against total impressions, or presents combined conversions without attribution scope notation is a fail, recorded with the specific columns present and the misread identified.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the columns present in the active report template, how viewability rate is calculated, and whether conversion attribution types are separated above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Yahoo DSP (demand-side platform) reporting template does not distinguish between total impressions and measurable impressions, or presents combined conversions without noting the view-through attribution share. Using measurable impressions as the total delivery figure overstates viewability rate because the measurable subset excludes impressions where measurement was technically unavailable. Presenting combined conversions without attribution context implies a level of click-driven engagement the campaign may not have produced. {{voice_recommend}} updating the standard report template to include separate columns for total impressions and measurable impressions, confirming that viewability rate is calculated as viewable impressions divided by measurable impressions, and adding a column or footnote distinguishing click-through from view-through conversions in any deliverable that quotes a conversion or return on ad spend figure.
